claim: Prompts are the primitive unit of agentic coding; context, model, prompt, and tools are the core four. Master these and you master all compositional features.
actionable: Before building skills, MCP servers, or sub-agents, master custom slash commands as your foundation; every advanced feature is built on prompt composition
source_title: I finally CRACKED Claude Agent Skills (Breakdown For Engineers)

claim: Skills are for packaging repeatable, reusable solutions; use them when one prompt is insufficient and you need to manage multiple tasks together (e.g., managing vs creating git worktrees)
actionable: Convert custom slash commands to skills only when you're solving a recurring MANAGEMENT problem, not one-off creation tasks; skills organize domain expertise
source_title: I finally CRACKED Claude Agent Skills (Breakdown For Engineers)

claim: MCP servers are for external integrations; skills can contain MCP servers but MCP servers should not wrap skills. MCP is a lower-level unit in the composition hierarchy.
actionable: Use MCP for third-party APIs and data sources; compose MCP servers INTO skills for domain-specific solutions, not the reverse
source_title: I finally CRACKED Claude Agent Skills (Breakdown For Engineers)

claim: Sub-agents isolate context windows and enable parallelization; use them for independent, isolatable tasks where you don't need context to persist after completion
actionable: When you need to run multiple complex tasks in parallel (e.g., security audit, code review), spawn sub-agents; accept the context loss at completion
source_title: I finally CRACKED Claude Agent Skills (Breakdown For Engineers)

claim: Custom slash commands are manual, explicit triggers; skills are agent-invoked automatic behaviors. This distinction determines when to choose each.
actionable: Use slash commands when you want to explicitly control when work happens; convert to skills when you want agents to autonomously decide when to apply the capability
source_title: I finally CRACKED Claude Agent Skills (Breakdown For Engineers)

claim: Progressive context disclosure in skills (metadata → instructions → resources on-demand) is more context-efficient than MCP servers which load everything on boot
actionable: Prefer skills over MCP for solving repeatable problems; skills avoid context window explosion by lazy-loading resources as agents need them
source_title: I finally CRACKED Claude Agent Skills (Breakdown For Engineers)

claim: Hooks enable deterministic automation at lifecycle events, balancing agent autonomy with explicit rules; combine hooks with agents (ADW pattern) for production reliability
actionable: Use hooks to enforce deterministic rules at specific points in your workflow; don't rely on agents alone for safety-critical automation
source_title: I finally CRACKED Claude Agent Skills (Breakdown For Engineers)

claim: Skills cannot yet nest sub-agents or custom slash commands directly; this is a limitation preventing fully composable file-system bundles like MCP
actionable: When designing skills, work around nested prompt/sub-agent limitations by composing them via tool calls; await official nesting support for full modularity
source_title: I finally CRACKED Claude Agent Skills (Breakdown For Engineers)

claim: Don't convert all slash commands to skills reflexively; this is a common mistake. Skills should manage recurring problem domains, not replace one-off prompts.
actionable: Keep one-time solutions as slash commands; only elevate to skills when you're repeatedly solving the same category of management problems
source_title: I finally CRACKED Claude Agent Skills (Breakdown For Engineers)
